main_ingredients: white fish fillets (typically grass carp or basa)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Spicy Boiled Fish is approximately 350 g, representing one individual portion from a shared dish, comprising fish fillets submerged in spiced chilli oil broth with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Shui Zhu Yu / Boiled Fish in Spicy Broth — composite nutrient estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked fish fillet, chilli oil, and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Sichuan spicy boiled fish as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: white fish fillet (basa/grass carp analogue using USDA catfish/tilapia entries), chilli oil, bean sprouts, and seasoning components. Proportions based on standard recipe composition (approximately 55% fish, 20% broth/oil, 15% vegetables, 10% seasoning). Energy and macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA regional fish dish data. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the as-served dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and mixed fish dishes; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate cooked fish dish category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sichuan spicy boiled fish. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal starchy carbohydrate (3.2 g/100g), consistent with a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (30 × 3.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 3.36, rounded to 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from doubanjiang, MSG, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Spicy Boiled Fish due to its high sodium content (approximately 480 mg/100g, equating to ~1680 mg per 350 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy Boiled Fish has a low glycemic index (estimated 30) and low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100g), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions and with attention to the high sodium content from the spiced broth.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and chilli oil fat content offset by beneficial fish protein and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fish component provides beneficial lean protein and polyunsaturated fatty acids, the high sodium content (~480 mg/100g) and significant oil content in the Sichuan broth raise cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Boiled Fish (沸腾鱼, Fèi téng yú), also known as Shui Zhu Yu (水煮鱼) in its closely related variant, is a signature Sichuan Chinese dish characterised by tender white fish fillets — typically grass carp, basa, or tilapia — submerged in a fiery, numbing broth of chilli oil, dried red chillies, and Sichuan peppercorns. The preparation involves marinating fish slices in egg white and starch, briefly blanching them in seasoned water, then pouring sizzling hot chilli oil over the assembled dish to finish cooking and release the aromatic compounds. The result is a dish with a bold, intensely spicy and numbing (málà) flavour profile, with silky-smooth fish texture contrasting against crunchy bean sprouts and other vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein, moderate-fat preparation (approximately 112 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 6.8 g fat per 100g) with low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100g), giving it a low glycemic index estimated at 30. The sodium content is high (~480 mg/100g) due to doubanjiang, MSG, and salt used in the broth, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and may contain soy and gluten allergens. In Singapore, it is served at Sichuan and Northern Chinese restaurants and is popular as a shared main course. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium restriction is advised for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.

Sodium content of Fei Teng Yu
What is the difference between Shui Zhu Yu and Fei Teng Yu?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sichuan spicy boiled fish as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for fish, chilli oil, and vegetable ingredients using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard recipe components per USDA FoodData Central. 3. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary ingredient and nutritional character of this dish is fish-based. 4. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' which is not applicable to this dish; this tag has been disregarded. 5. GI set to 30 as an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate cooked fish dish; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. Halal status is marked false as doubanjiang (fermented bean paste) and MSG sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-specific verification. 7. The dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay, Singapore) per the source field survey record; this is a Northern Chinese restaurant context, though the dish itself is Sichuan in origin.
